metric_value: Integrated cross-application ILM software is 4-5 years from practical enterprise-wide maturity (circa 2008-2009)
notes: Author's central thesis; waiting for maturity is discouraged

metric_value: SANs supplemented with NAS now affordable for even small enterprises
notes: Step 1 prerequisite: centralize storage before ILM can function

metric_value: Midline disk at approximately 25% of FC/SCSI cost for moderate random access workloads
notes: Key economic driver for migration of reference data off expensive FC/SCSI

metric_value: More than 50% of enterprise data is semi-structured or unstructured and seldom changing
notes: Supports argument for midline disk adoption
Cited as driver for swing toward midline disk for reference data

metric_value: Midline disk-to-disk backup compresses backup times by up to 50% vs tape
Offline tapes represent an enormous liability in litigation; data retention policy adherence required
notes: Compliance and legal risk associated with tape retention

metric_value: Every application platform changes within 5 years; use refresh points to migrate data to proper pool
notes: Step 4 premise: no forced platform migration before 5-year refresh point

metric_value: Rising terabytes-per-admin ratio as virtualization becomes embedded and automation reduces intervention
Storage-related hours on production systems reduced by 80% when ILM matures circa 2008-2010
notes: Step 6 outcome prediction; full legal/audit compliance sign-off prerequisite

metric_value: FC/SCSI growth projected to be modest as midline pool absorbs workloads previously on expensive disks
notes: Step 5: expected shift in spending mix

metric_value: Six steps must be taken in order; each step implies existence of the prior one
notes: Sequential dependency noted; not necessary enterprise-wide, but within IT org experience

metric_value: Increasing number of IT professionals standardizing on four-pool storage model
notes: Four pools: online FC/SCSI, midline, nearline buffered, offline tape

metric_value: Systematically analyzing which pool data belongs in is messy but enormously beneficial
notes: Three data classification axes: type (structured/semi/unstructured), use (active/fixed), pool
